sections.heading: Demand was nearly unchanged, but modern methods carried more of the met need
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, total demand for family planning was 41.4 percent in 2025, almost the same as 41.8 percent in 2022. Met need through all methods was also essentially flat, at 33.8 percent in 2025 compared with 34.1 percent in 2022. The clearer change was inside met need: modern methods rose from 24.3 percent to 25.7 percent, while unmet need stayed almost unchanged at 7.6 percent.

sections.heading: Demand is highest in the middle reproductive ages
sections.body: Total demand rises sharply after the teenage years, from 6.0 percent at ages 15 to 19 to 46.6 percent at ages 25 to 29. It peaks at 63.0 percent among women aged 35 to 39, with met need through all methods also highest in that group at 53.9 percent. Unmet need is more compressed across the main reproductive ages, ranging from 7.1 percent at ages 45 to 49 to 10.3 percent at ages 25 to 29.

sections.heading: The rural-urban gap is visible but not the main divide
sections.body: Rural women had higher total demand than urban women in 2025, 44.4 percent compared with 39.1 percent. Rural women also had higher met need through all methods, 36.3 percent compared with 31.9 percent, and higher modern-method met need, 28.8 percent compared with 23.3 percent. Unmet need differed less sharply, at 8.1 percent in rural areas and 7.2 percent in urban areas.

sections.heading: Education does not form a simple ladder
sections.body: Family planning demand is not lowest among women with the least schooling or highest among women with the most schooling. In 2025, total demand was highest among women with Grades 1 to 6 education at 59.6 percent and post-secondary education at 56.1 percent, while it was much lower among women with Grades 11 to 12 at 20.4 percent. Unmet need was highest among women with no education at 13.9 percent and Grades 1 to 6 at 11.7 percent, even though those groups had very different levels of met need.

sections.heading: Lower-wealth groups show higher demand and higher unmet need
sections.body: Total demand was highest in the lowest wealth quintile at 50.7 percent and declined to 34.5 percent in the highest quintile. Met need through all methods followed the same broad direction, from 39.6 percent in the lowest quintile to 28.4 percent in the highest. Unmet need also remained higher at the bottom of the wealth distribution, at 11.2 percent in the lowest quintile compared with 6.1 percent in the highest.

sections.heading: BARMM is the clearest regional outlier
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest met need through all methods in 2025, at 16.2 percent, and the highest unmet need, at 18.5 percent. Its total demand was 34.8 percent, close to several other lower-demand regions, but the balance between met and unmet need was very different. Davao Region, by contrast, had the highest met need through all methods at 45.1 percent and much lower unmet need at 6.2 percent.

sections.heading: Modern-method met need separates several southern regions from the rest
sections.body: The highest modern-method met need in 2025 was in Davao Region at 37.1 percent, followed by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. These regions were well above the national modern-method level of 25.7 percent. BARMM was at the opposite end at 13.0 percent, making the regional spread in modern-method met need larger than the rural-urban gap.

sections.heading: Some regions moved in opposite directions from the national stability
sections.body: National total demand changed little between 2022 and 2025, but several regions did not follow that pattern. Central Visayas fell from 45.9 percent to 35.5 percent in total demand, while its met need through all methods dropped from 37.0 percent to 25.7 percent. Eastern Visayas moved the other way, with total demand rising from 41.3 percent to 46.8 percent and met need through all methods rising from 35.6 percent to 39.7 percent.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula combines high unmet need with only midrange met need
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had unmet need of 11.0 percent in 2025, higher than the national level of 7.6 percent. Its met need through all methods was 30.2 percent, below the national level of 33.8 percent, while total demand was close to the national level at 41.2 percent. The pattern points to a region where demand is not unusually high overall, but a larger share of that demand remains unmet.

takeaways: Family planning demand among all women was stable nationally, but modern methods made up more of the met need in 2025.
Regional variation is sharper than the rural-urban divide, especially for modern-method met need and unmet need.
BARMM stands out because unmet need is high while met need is low, a different pattern from regions where demand is simply lower.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
